title: Add Logic To C# Console Applications - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of if/else, switch statements, ternary operator, logical operators, loops, break/continue in C#

questions.question: Which C# statement is used to execute one block of code when a condition is true and another when it is false?
questions.explanation: The `if/else` statement evaluates a Boolean expression and executes one block if true and another if false. Example: `if (score >= 70) { Console.WriteLine("Pass"); } else { Console.WriteLine("Fail"); }`
The `&&` (logical AND) operator requires both conditions to be true. Since 10 > 5 is true AND 10 < 15 is true, the overall condition is true, so "In range" is printed.

questions.question: Which C# keyword is used to test a variable against multiple constant values?
questions.explanation: The `switch` statement tests a variable against multiple `case` values. It's cleaner than a long chain of if/else if statements when comparing a single variable against many constant values.

questions.question: What is the C# ternary operator syntax?
questions.explanation: The ternary (conditional) operator uses the syntax `condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse`. Example: `string result = score >= 70 ? "Pass" : "Fail";`

questions.question: What is the difference between a `while` loop and a `do-while` loop in C#?

options.text: A do-while loop always executes its body at least once
A while loop always executes its body at least once
A do-while loop can only iterate a fixed number of times

questions.explanation: A `do-while` loop checks the condition AFTER executing the body, guaranteeing at least one execution. A `while` loop checks the condition BEFORE, so the body may never execute if the condition is initially false.

questions.question: What does the `break` statement do inside a loop?

options.text: Skips to the next iteration of the loop
Restarts the loop from the beginning

questions.explanation: The `break` statement immediately exits the innermost loop it's contained in. Execution continues with the first statement after the loop. Use `continue` to skip to the next iteration instead.

questions.question: Which loop is best suited for iterating over each element in an array in C#?
questions.explanation: The `foreach` loop iterates over each element in a collection or array without needing an index variable. Example: `foreach (string name in names) { Console.WriteLine(name); }`. It's cleaner and less error-prone for simple iteration.

questions.question: What is the output of: `Console.WriteLine(true || false);`?
questions.explanation: The `||` (logical OR) operator returns true if at least one operand is true. Since the first operand is true, the result is true. In C#, Boolean values print as "True" or "False" (capitalized).

questions.question: In a `for` loop `for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)`, how many times does the loop body execute?

options.text: It depends on the loop body

questions.explanation: The loop starts with i = 0 and runs while i < 5, incrementing i after each iteration. So i takes values 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — the body executes 5 times.

questions.question: What does the `!` (logical NOT) operator do in C#?

options.text: Inverts a Boolean value (true becomes false, false becomes true)

questions.explanation: The `!` operator is the logical NOT operator. It inverts a Boolean value: `!true` evaluates to `false`, and `!false` evaluates to `true`. It's commonly used to negate conditions in if statements.
